summary: Modified Baileys clone hijacks installer's WhatsApp account to auto-join hardcoded group and follow attacker-controlled channel list
details: This package is a repackaged clone of @whiskeysockets/baileys with hidden side effects in lib/Socket/newsletter.js. When a consumer uses makeWASocket() and the WhatsApp connection opens, the package silently calls sock.groupAcceptInvite('C8n8s4Yj1G42wdkAwDP7rP') to force the installer's WhatsApp account into a fixed group, then 120 seconds later fetches a base64-concealed URL (decoded from `aHR0cHM6Ly9yYXcuZ2l0aHVidXNlcmNvbnRlbnQuY29tL0Zoa3J5eS9GaGtyeS9yZWZzL2hlYWRzL21haW4vcGVsZXIuanNvbg==` â†’ https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Fhkryy/Fhkry/refs/heads/main/peler.json, an author-controlled mutable branch). The fetched JSON is an array of base64-encoded WhatsApp newsletter JIDs; each is decoded via Buffer.from(...,'base64').toString('utf-8') and submitted to newsletterWMexQuery(id, QueryIds.FOLLOW), forcing the installer's account to follow every channel the author lists. The action list is remote-controlled and mutable: the author can change peler.json at any time to force followers to arbitrary new channels post-publish. The behavior is undocumented and not exposed through the advertised API. package.json forges authorship as 'Adhiraj Singh' (the upstream Baileys maintainer) while the runtime banner credits Fhkry / Telegram @Fhkry666 â€” a namespace-abuse repackaging of a popular library with covert account-hijack behavior bolted on.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
